title: SparseGPT: Massive Language Models Can be Accurately Pruned in One-Shot

chunks.text:
We show for the ﬁrst time that large-scale genera-
tive pretrained transformer (GPT) family mod-
els can be pruned to at least 50% sparsity in
one-shot, without any retraining, at minimal
loss of accuracy.
This is achieved via a new
pruning method called SparseGPT, speciﬁcally
designed to work efﬁciently and accurately on
massive GPT-family models. We can execute
SparseGPT on the largest available open-source
models, OPT-175B and BLOOM-176B, in un-
der 4.5 hours, and can reach 60% unstructured
sparsity with negligible increase in perplexity: re-
markably, more than 100 billion weights from
these models can be ignored at inference time.
SparseGPT generalizes to semi-structured (2:4
and 4:8) patterns, and is compatible with weight
quantization approaches. The code is available
at: https://github.com/IST-DASLab/
sparsegpt.

Large Language Models (LLMs) from the Generative Pre-
trained Transformer (GPT) family have shown remarkable
performance on a wide range of tasks, but are difﬁcult to de-
ploy because of their massive size and computational costs.
For illustration, the top-performing GPT-175B models have

We have provided a new post-training pruning method
called SparseGPT, speciﬁcally tailored to massive lan-
guage models from the GPT family. Our results show for the
ﬁrst time that large-scale generative pretrained Transformer-
family models can be compressed to high sparsity via weight
pruning in one-shot, without any retraining, at low loss of
accuracy, when measured both in terms of perplexity and

SparseGPT: Massive Language Models Can be Accurately Pruned in One-Shot
zero-shot performance. Speciﬁcally, we have shown that
the largest open-source GPT-family models (e.g. OPT-175B
and BLOOM-176B) can reach 50-60% sparsity, dropping
more than 100B weights, with low accuracy ﬂuctuations.
Our work shows that the high degree of parametrization
of massive GPT models allows pruning to directly identify
sparse accurate models in the “close neighborhood” of the
dense model, without gradient information. Remarkably, the
output of such sparse models correlates extremely closely
with that of the dense model. We also show that larger
models are easier to sparsify: at a ﬁxed sparsity level, the
relative accuracy drop for the larger sparse models narrows
as we increase model size, to the point where inducing 50%
sparsity results in practically no accuracy decrease on the
largest models, which should be seen as very encouraging
for future work on compressing such massive models.
7. Acknowledgements
The authors gratefully acknowledge funding from the Euro-
pean Research Council (ERC) under the European Union’s
Horizon 2020 programme (grant agreement No. 805223
ScaleML), as well as experimental support from Eldar Kur-
tic, and from the IST Austria IT department, in particular
Stefano Elefante, Andrei Hornoiu, and Alois Schloegl.

classification.reasoning: The paper addresses the high computational and memory cost of running very large GPT models by introducing a one‑shot pruning technique that creates sparse versions with 50‑60% unstructured sparsity, directly reducing inference FLOPs and memory without retraining. Its primary aim is to make existing massive models cheaper to deploy, not to increase their capability or train larger models. The only scaling‑related aspect is that larger models prune more easily, but this is a side observation rather than a goal of achieving more capable models.
classification.justification: SparseGPT contributes a post‑training pruning method that yields highly sparse, inference‑efficient versions of OPT‑175B and BLOOM‑176B, enabling faster, lower‑memory inference. This aligns with the efficiency objective of reducing the cost of running LLMs at their current scale. It does not propose new training regimes, data scaling, or capability improvements, so it does not fit the scaling category. The work directly answers the professor’s question about making LLMs more efficient.

stage1.reasoning: The paper introduces SparseGPT, a one-shot pruning technique that compresses large GPT models to high sparsity, directly reducing inference cost and memory, thus focusing on efficiency.
